source_metadata.title_raw: Samantha W. Zutler - I-18-049 - April 17, 2018 - Oakland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 17, 2018

Samantha W. Zutler
Burke Williams & Sorensen, LLP
1701 Harrison Street, Suite 900
Oakland, CA 94612-3501

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-18-049

Dear Ms. Zutler:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Healdsburg's Vice Mayor David Hagele regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.³

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May the Vice Mayor take part in an agreement between the City Council and a developer regarding a housing project, if he has received commission income from the client of $500 or more in the 12 months before the decision as a real estate broker, but does not have an ongoing compensation agreement with the client and has not received income related to the project before the City Council?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

³ You did not include a request for advice under Government Code Section 1090. Accordingly, we have not analyzed Section 1090's impact on the provided facts. Please contact us for further analysis if you would like advice regarding Section 1090.

File No. I-18-049
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

No. The Vice Mayor is disqualified from taking part in the agreement. Even if the income was not received for the project before the City Council, the Vice Mayor may not take part in any agreement between the City Council and a developer if the Vice Mayor has received income from the developer aggregating $500 within 12 months prior to the decision, including commission income received from the developer as a real estate broker.⁴

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

Vice Mayor David Hagele is a licensed real estate broker who has sole ownership of a business that provides brokerage services and arranges commercial real estate loans throughout the state of California. Prior to the close of escrow on a property, his business typically submits an invoice to escrow for its fee, which is based on a percentage of the final loan amount. The commission he receives for such services is paid to the Vice Mayor's brokerage business, and typically exceeds $500 for any given loan arranged by the Vice Mayor.

The Vice Mayor has arranged several loans for local developers, including a developer bringing a project before the City Council for approval of a regulatory agreement regarding a housing project.

In light of his business activity, the Vice Mayor asks whether he may take part in City Council decisions involving development projects if he has received commission income of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision from the developer for a project not before the City Council, does not have an ongoing compensation agreement with the developer, and has not received any income for the project before the City Council.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise, including the following which are relevant to the question presented:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

_______________
⁴ Please note that when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

File No. I-18-049
Page No. 3

• An interest in any source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. An official
always has an interest in his or her personal finances.5

Under the provided facts, the Vice Mayor has a business interest in his brokerage business
as its sole owner. The Vice Mayor also has source of income interests from his brokerage business.
Most pertinent to the question, as the sole owner of his brokerage business, the Vice Mayor also has
a source of income in any client if his business has received income aggregating to $500 or more
within the 12 months prior to the decision. Under Regulation 18700.1(c)(1), "commission" income
received as a real estate broker can be attributed to multiple sources for purposes of disqualification
including the client represented in the transaction.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Standards for foreseeability vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) A financial interest is
at issue if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest. For
a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the
decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

For sources of income, Regulation 18702.3 provides that a decision's financial effect is
material if the source of income is a business entity that will be financially affected under the
standards applied to business interests, which are set forth in Regulation 18702.1. Regulation
18702.1 establishes that the financial effect of a decision on a business entity, in which an official
has a financial interest, is material whenever the business entity:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government action
concerning the business entity;

[¶] … [¶]

"(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency;

[¶] … [¶]

_________________________
5 An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members is not
considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation
18702.5(c).) Because the decision may affect your business interest, we do not analyze your interest in your personal
finances.

File No. 1-18-049
Page No. 4

"(5) Applies for a permit, license, grant, tax credit, exception, variance, or
other entitlement that the official's agency is authorized to issue;

[¶] . . . [¶]

"(7) Is otherwise subject to an action taken by the official's agency, the effect
of which is directed solely at the business entity in which the official has an
interest."

The decision before the City Council is whether to approve an agreement with a developer
regarding the developer's project. As a named party in the agreement, and therefore the decision,
the developer is explicitly involved in the decision and any financial effect of the decision is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable. Moreover, the developer has initiated the proceeding in
proposing the project and any agreement is a contract between the developer and the official's
agency, an application for an entitlement that the City Council is authorized to issue, and directed
solely at the developer. Therefore, the financial effect is material under Regulation 18702.1(a)(1),
(3), (5), and (7).

Any City Council decision regarding an agreement with a developer has a reasonably
foreseeable and material effect on the Vice Mayor's interest in the developer as a source of income.
Even if the income was not received for the project before the City Council, the Vice Mayor may
not take part in City Council's decisions regarding an agreement with a developer if the Vice Mayor
has received income from the developer aggregating $500 within 12 months prior to the decision,
including commission income received from the developer as a real estate broker.⁶

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:jgl

⁶ Because we conclude that the Vice Mayor has a conflict of interest based on his source-of-income interest in
the developer, we do not analyze his other financial interests.
